title: Campaign finance
description: Practice your skills in this quiz on how campaign finance affects the electoral process

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement accurately summarizes the impact of the *Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission* (2010) decision?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It allows corporations and unions to spend unlimited amounts of money on political activities that are independent of a party or candidate
It allows political campaign contributions through political action committees (PACs) to be tax deductible
It allows individual donors to give unlimited amounts of money directly to specific federal candidates or campaigns
It allows political action committees (PACS) to spend unlimited amounts of money on individual candidate campaigns

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best summarizes a central criticism in the ongoing debate surrounding the Supreme Court’s ruling in *Citizens United v. Federal Election Commission* (2010)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The Supreme Court has extended First Amendment protection of political speech at the expense of election fairness
Limits placed on election spending are unconstitutional and reduce the quantity of expression that is necessary in elections
The Federal Election Commission has reduced the amount of “hard money” campaign contributions, resulting in a decrease in voter registrations and turnout
The “Stand by Your Ad” provision has polarized communities now that candidates and parties are required to approve campaign ads

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the image below to answer the following question. 

![](${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2ae2df7427d18b22ac1173280baaed36.svg)

*Source: Data from [Opensecrets.org](https://www.opensecrets.org/overview/donordemographics.php?cycle=2010&filter=A)*

**Based on the information in the graphic, which of the following claims would an opponent to the decision in *Citizens United v. Federal Elections Commission* (2010) most likely make? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Campaign finance laws allow a small proportion of the US population to influence elections unfairly through unlimited political contributions
Campaign finance laws are sufficient and have not given any person an unfair advantage in the amount a person can donate
Voter turnout will likely increase by 32% as campaign contributions and campaign advertisements increase
Voters who do not donate to election campaigns are likely to exert influence through channels other than campaign contributions

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following was a provision of the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (BCRA) of 2002?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It banned national political parties from raising and spending soft money.
It permitted unlimited independent spending by corporations and labor unions.
It prohibited individuals from contributing directly to political parties.
It eliminated limits on campaign spending for congressional candidates.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of the political impact of both policy-creating decisions?**

 | Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act (BCRA) | *Citizens United v. Federal Election Committee* (2010)
- | - | -
**A** | Stand by your Ad provision  | Limitations on “hard money” contributions
**B** | Created the Federal Election Commission | Labor and corporate spending on party-building activities are protected speech
**C** |Political spending is a form of constitutionally protected speech | Strengthened truth-in-advertising laws regarding political advertising
**D** | Decreased role of “soft money” in political campaigns | First Amendment allows corporations and unions to fund independent political advertising

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
A campaign advertisement appears on TV and in social media that says, “Candidate Smith has a record that includes policies that will hurt all Americans. If these policies are put into place, much of your hard-earned savings will be taxed away, and your well-paying job will be moved overseas. Be sure to vote on election day."

**Which statement below best explains what limitations, if any, might be placed on this campaign message?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Smith’s opponent could use “soft money” to pay for the ad and does not have to claim support for it
Smith’s opponent would have to use personal funds to pay for the ad, which must include hard evidence to support the accusation
Smith’s opponent would have to use “hard money” to pay for the ad and could be sued for running it
Smith’s opponent would use “hard money” to pay for the ad, since it attacks a political candidate without telling who paid for it

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which of the following is an accurate comparison of the political impact of both policy-creating decisions?**

 | *Schenck v. United States* (1919) | *Citizens United v. Federal Election Committee* (2010)
- | - | -
**A** | Ruled that speech that posed a ‘clear and present danger’ was not protected  | Ruled that the federal government could restrict campaign spending by corporations, but not labor
**B** | Applied the First Amendment to the states | Applied the Fifth Amendment to the states
**C** | Led to an increase in power for the national government to restrict freedom of speech | Led to the creation of independent expenditure-only committees (Super PACs)
**D** | Declared that wearing a black armband to protest the Vietnam War was a form of symbolic speech | Declared that political spending was a form of protected free speech

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Comparison A
Comparison B
Comparison C
Comparison D

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the table below to answer the following question. 

**Sector Totals, 2017-2018**

Rank | Sector | Amount | Total to Parties and Candidates | Dems Percent | GOP Percent
- | - | - | -| -
**1** | Finance/Insurance/Real Estate |\$260,587,304 |\$198,225,834 | 42.0% | 57.8% 
**2** | Other | \$187,090,094 | \$160,961,296 | 60.9% |38.5%
**3** | Ideology/Single-issue | \$175,264,839 |\$130,280,835| 62.8% |36.5% 
**4** | Misc. Business |\$127,865,840 |\$95,482,285 | 43.7% | 55.8% 
**5** |Lawyers and Lobbyists | \$90,343,168 |\$85,392,646| 67.9% | 31.7%
**6** | Health | \$75,355,156  |\$69,684,540 | 50.1% | 49.6%
**7** | Communication/Electronics  | \$64,720,145 |\$53,809,226 | 63.0% | 36.3%
**8** | Energy/Natural Resources | \$55,707,326  |\$39,671,761 | 20.5% | 79.4%
**9** | Labor | \$55,582,848  |\$29,232,259| 79.0% | 20.5%
**10** | Agribusiness | \$34,711,079  | \$31,776,340 | 28.4% | 71.1% 

Source: [OpenSecrets.org](https://www.opensecrets.org/overview/sectors.php), based on data released by the FEC on Monday, March 26, 2018

**Which two interest group sectors in the chart show the greatest percentage difference between their contributions to each of the two major parties?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Energy/Natural Resources and Labor
Ideology/Single Issue and Health
Finance/Insurance/Real Estate and Communications/Electronics
Lawyers/Lobbyists and Agribusiness

**Which best explains why the health sector showed almost an equal percentage of contributions to both major parties in this period?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They gave equally to both sides to retain influence with whichever party holds each chamber of Congress and the presidency
They wanted to fund those who supported President Trump’s health care plan
They wanted to ensure both parties have the same capital to recruit the best candidates and develop the best policies on health care
They made contributions based on candidates’  liberal and conservative positions on healthcare rather than their party affiliations

**Contributions listed in the chart above include “hard” and “soft” money donations. Which best explains the difference between hard and soft money contributions?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Hard money donations go straight to the party or candidate, while soft money can only be used for general “party-building” activities
Hard money refers to cash donations, while soft money refers to other types of assets such as stocks, bonds, or real estate
Hard money donations must go to voter education funding,  while soft money contributions can go to parties and individual candidates
Hard money donations come from industrial group sectors, while soft money is contributed from professional and service sectors

assessment_items.question_content:
A journalist writes a critical article about a new political action committee (PAC) that raised over $20 million in a single month to support one Senate candidate. The article argues that such spending undermines the idea of a fair election by giving wealthy donors too much influence. In response, the PAC's director claims that their spending is a legitimate expression of political views and should not be restricted by the government. 

**What is the best explanation of the constitutional issue raised by the scenario?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Limiting political spending may violate the First Amendment’s protection of free speech.
Restricting political donations may interfere with the First Amendment’s right to petition the government.
Limiting access to political advertising may violate the First Amendment’s protection of a free press.
Requiring public disclosure of donors may violate the First Amendment’s right to freely associate.

assessment_items.question_content:
Use the table below to answer the following question. 

**Contribution limits for 2017-2018 federal elections**

Donor |Candidate committee | PAC | Party committee: state/district/local | Party committee: nation | Additional national party committee accounts 
- | - | - | -| -| -
**Individual** | \$2,700* per election |\$5,000 per year |\$10,000 per year (combined) | \$33,900* per year | \$101,700* per account, per year 
**Candidate committee** | \$2,000 per election | \$5,000 per year | Unlimited transfers | Unlimited transfers | -
**PAC: multicandidate** | \$5,000 per election | \$5,000 per year |\$5,000 per year (combined)| \$15,000 per year | \$45,000 per account, per year 
**PAC: nonmulticandidate** | \$2,700* per election |\$5,000 per year |\$10,000 per year (combined) | \$33,900* per year | \$101,700* per account, per year 
**Party committee: state/district/local** |\$5,000 per election | \$5,000 per year | Unlimited transfers| Unlimited transfers | -
**Party committee: national** | \$5,000 per election | \$5,000 per year  | Unlimited transfers| Unlimited transfers | -


**Note:** (*) Indexed for inflation in odd-numbered years 

Source: [Federal Election Commission](https://transition.fec.gov/pages/brochures/contriblimitschart.htm)

**Based on the Federal Election Commission’s chart, how do contribution limits from non-multicandidate political action committees compare with those from individual donors?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They are the same as those for individual contributors across all types of recipient
Contributions from non-multicandidate PACs are slightly lower for candidate committees compared to individual donor limits, and no limits at all on transfers to a national party committee
Non-multicandidate PACs are allowed unlimited transfers to state/district/local party committees, while individuals are limited to \$10,000 to these same committees
Non-multicandidate PACs may give  almost double what individuals can contribute to a candidate committee, but half as much as individuals can give to state and local party committees

**Which type of organization is specifically excluded from the Federal Election Commission’s contribution limitations chart, based on Supreme Court and appellate First Amendment rulings in the 2010s?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Independent-expenditure-only political committees or super PACs
Corporations, including nonprofits
Labor unions
Political action committees or PACs

**Which of the following Supreme Court cases is most relevant to the topic of the cartoon?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: *Citizens United v. Federal Elections Commission* (2010)
*Baker v. Carr* (1962)
*Shaw v. Reno* (1993)
*McDonald v. Chicago* (2010)

assessment_items.question_content:
**What is the best explanation for how super PACs differ from traditional PACs (political action committees)?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Super PACs can raise and spend unlimited money, as long as they act independently of candidates and campaigns.
Super PACs are allowed to contribute money directly to candidates, unlike traditional PACs.
Super PACs must follow strict contribution limits, but traditional PACs do not have those restrictions.
Super PACs are not permitted to make any political expenditures, while traditional PACs can spend money on campaigns.
